coredata.dc:title: Ultratrace and robust visual sensor of Cd2+ ions based on the size-dependent optical properties of Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles in mice models
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zhuoyue
Zhang, Zhen
Liu, Huihui
Mao, Xiang
Liu, Wei
Zhang, Shouting
Nie, Zongxiu
Lu, Xiaoquan
coredata.dc:description: Visual inspection is expected as an ideal technique, which can directly and conveniently detect heavy metal ions by observing the color change. Insensitivity of detecting weakly colored heavy transition metal ions and low adsorptivity of metal ions on nanoparticle surface are two main factors hindering the application of visual detection in heavy metal ions detection. Herein, we demonstrated an operational colorimetric sensor based on the color dependence of nanoparticles aggregation to selective and facile detect weakly colored transition heavy metal Cd2+ ions that have been considered as the origin of the “Itai-itai” disease. Uniform colloidal 15nm graphite-like nitride doped carbon quantum dots-capped gold nanoparticle (Au@g-CNQDs) was successfully prepared, wherein the existence of numerous heptazine, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the nanoparticle's surface strengthened adsorption of the Cd2+ ions on the surface of Au@g-CNQDs through the “cooperative effect”. As a consequence, without expensive and intricate exogenous indicators or other special additives, the Cd2+ ions could sensitively and quickly captured to detect at ultra-low concentration within 30s by the naked-eye. Under the optimal conditions, the Cd2+ ions sensor possesses good analytical performances with a wide linear range of 0.01–3.0μM and a detection limit of 10nM (S/N = 3). Moreover, the biodistribution and aggregation of Cd2+ ions were detected effectively in mice organ tissues suggesting its great potential use for real-word applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphite-like nitride doped carbon quantum dots (g-CNQDs)
Visual sensor
Color dependence
Cd2+ ions detection
Tissues
Biodistribution

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric detection of Cd2+ ions in aqueous solutions
Study on Cd2+ ions biodistribution in mice models
Characterization of the g-CNQDs, Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles
The mechanism of the visual detection of Cd2+ ions
Biological test in mice organ tissues

Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21575115 , 21327005 and 21705117 ); Program for Chang Jiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team, Ministry of Education, China (Grant No. IRT −16R61 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image (scale bar 20nm) of g-CNQDs and corresponding HR-TEM images (scale bar 2nm); (b) The TEM images of Au@g-CNQDs and corresponding particle size distribution of Au NPs; (c) FTIR spectra of the g-CNQDs and Au@g-CNQDs; (d) The HR-TEM image of Au@g-CNQDs and the corresponded Au, C and N elements mapping; (e) XPS spectra of Au@g-CNQDs and High-resolution XPS data of N1s of Au@g-CNQDs (inset); (f) UV–visible absorption spectra of g-CNQDs and Au@g-CNQDs aqueous solution; FL spectrum of g-CNQDs and Au@g-CNQDs.

(a) Cd (3d) and (b) N (11s) XPS spectra of Au@g-CNQDs and Cd/Au@g-CNQDs, the experimental spetra are shown as solid curves, and the fitting data are shown in dotted curves; (c) Schematic of Cd2+ ions induced Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles aggregation; (d) TEM images of Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles in the absence of Cd2+ ions and (e) TEM images of Au@g-CNQDs in the presence of 1mMCd2+ ions; (f) UV–vis spectra of Au@g-CNQDs and Cd/Au@g-CNQDs recorded.

(a) Selectively detection of Cd2+ ions to other ions, including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Cr6+ and Hg2+ions, in the presence of 0.1μMCd2+ ions; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of Au@g-CNQDs in the presence of different concentration of NaCl; (c) Response (A650/A520) of the colorimetric assay of Au@g-CNQDs solutions in the presence of several competing metal ions includes in the absence and presence of Cd2+ ions; (d) The changes of UV–vis absorption spectra after the addition of Cd2+; (e) The color change and the different absorption ratio at A650/A520 versus different Cd2+ concentration; (f) The linearity curve of the ratio of A650/A520 versus Cd2+ concentration; Experiment conditions: tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.0), NaCl (15mM), incubation time (2min).

The biodistribution of Cd2+ ions in various tissues: (a) Liver, (b) kidney, and (c) lung were acquired from mice at 0, 1, 3, and 5 d postinjection of 0.01% (100μL) of Cd2+ ions. Quantitative analysis of Cd2+ ions in liver, kidney, and lung was made using UV–vis spectrophotometer. (Scale bar = 100µm).
Strategy for the colorimetric detection of Cd2+ ions using Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles.
Quantitative determination of Cd2+ in real environmental water samples.

head.ce:title: Ultratrace and robust visual sensor of Cd2+ ions based on the size-dependent optical properties of Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles in mice models

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou 730070, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Bioelectrochemistry & Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Visual inspection is expected as an ideal technique, which can directly and conveniently detect heavy metal ions by observing the color change. Insensitivity of detecting weakly colored heavy transition metal ions and low adsorptivity of metal ions on nanoparticle surface are two main factors hindering the application of visual detection in heavy metal ions detection. Herein, we demonstrated an operational colorimetric sensor based on the color dependence of nanoparticles aggregation to selective and facile detect weakly colored transition heavy metal Cd2+ ions that have been considered as the origin of the “Itai-itai” disease. Uniform colloidal 15nm graphite-like nitride doped carbon quantum dots-capped gold nanoparticle (Au@g-CNQDs) was successfully prepared, wherein the existence of numerous heptazine, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the nanoparticle's surface strengthened adsorption of the Cd2+ ions on the surface of Au@g-CNQDs through the “cooperative effect”. As a consequence, without expensive and intricate exogenous indicators or other special additives, the Cd2+ ions could sensitively and quickly captured to detect at ultra-low concentration within 30s by the naked-eye. Under the optimal conditions, the Cd2+ ions sensor possesses good analytical performances with a wide linear range of 0.01–3.0μM and a detection limit of 10nM (S/N = 3). Moreover, the biodistribution and aggregation of Cd2+ ions were detected effectively in mice organ tissues suggesting its great potential use for real-word applications.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Graphite-like nitride doped carbon quantum dots (g-CNQDs)

ce:section.ce:para: Rapidly detection of the non-degraded and easy accumulated heavy metal ions with high sensitivity is of significant importance for environmental monitoring, food security, drug analysis, and clinical diagnosis (Barton et al., 2015; Krstic et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2017). Various analytical techniques, such as mass spectrometry (Hu et al., 2017), atomic absorption spectrometry (Dasbasi et al., 2016), fluorescence spectrometry (Wu et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2016), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (Liu et al., 2017), and atomic emission spectrometry (Sengupta et al., 2015) have been employed to detect heavy metal ions. The challenge comes as how to detect low concentration of heavy metal ions in the real samples containing multiple types of heavy metal ions, which generally requires intensive and complex analytical procedures.

The eye is considered as the most important organ of human in information which can identify different objects through simply observing the change of color, shape, state, etc (Li et al., 2014). Benefited from the quick and sensitive character of capturing color variations, several visual inspection schemes were then prevailing for heavy metal ions detection. For example, Wang et al. developed a catalysis-based analysis methodology concept for detecting Hg2+ ions in blood and wastewater, wherein the catalysis activity of gel-AgNPs (Ag nanoparticles) greatly enhanced by Hg2+ ions leading to the effective oxidation and color change of TMB (3, 3′, 5, 5′ -tetramethylbenzidine) (Sun et al., 2014). Zeng et al. demonstrated a colorimetric change platform to detection of Cu2+ ions via the Cu+-catalyzed click chemistry and hemin/G-quadruplex HRP-mimicking DNAzyme to catalyze the colorless TMB into a colored product (Ge et al., 2014). Tang et al. designed a facile colorimetric detection of Ag+ ions by tuning the peroxidase-like activity of the Pt cubes (Gao et al., 2017). Our previous work also shown that Hg2+ ions can be ultrasensitive detected with the naked-eye through the mercury-stimulated peroxidase mimetic activity of rGO/PEI/Pd (reduced graphene oxide/polyethylenimine/Pd), where the oxidation was obvious promoted by the Hg2+ ions inducing the color of TMB in solution changed to dark blue (Zhang et al., 2017a).

Notably, those sensing methods aim at using the specific heavy metal ions stimulated enzyme mimetic activity to oxidation and color change of the zymolyte, thus realizing the detection of the corresponding heavy metal ions (Annadhasan and Rajendiran, 2015; Zhi et al., 2016). Therefore, the current visual inspection of metal ions was mainly confined in catalytic metal ions, resulting in the detectable ions restricted. Many weak catalytic metal ions enable to severely threat to health.(Goswami et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2012). A classic example is the Cd2+ ions, which is an important toxic heavy metal ion (Wang et al., 2017). As it is not metabolized or decomposed and poorly excreted, very low concentration of Cd2+ ions can lead to the“Itai-itai”disease (Shinya Hayasaka et al., 2003). Even worse, through the bio-enrichment of Cd2+ ions from soils to vegetables and grain, waste water to mammal, etc., Cd2+ ions can easily enter human body and accumulate at vital organ like kidney, spleen, lung and liver, seriously endangering the public health (Manjumeena et al., 2015). Therefore, it is of great significance to study a robust and convenient visual detection method to detect Cd2+ along the food chain (Uchimiya et al., 2010).

Attributed to the collective oscillation of the free conduction electrons that induced by the interacting electromagnetic field, the simple synthesized and easy surface-modified gold nanoparticles possess unique optical property (Song et al., 2016). The appearing color of the gold nanoparticle solution is determined by the nanoparticles' size and shape (Callegari et al., 2003; Blandez et al., 2015; Dong et al., 2013). For small gold spherical nanoparticles with diameters of 2R < 25nm (Xiao-Hong Nancy Xu et al., 2004; Juan and Evangelina, 1998), the position of the absorption maximum size increases with increasing the particle size, according to the Mie theory equation
κ =
18 π N V
ϵ
2
ϵ
m
3 / 2
/
{
λ ×
[
(
ϵ
1
+
2
ϵ
m
)
2
+
ϵ
2
2
]
}
, where
κ
is the extinction coefficient, N is the number of particle, V is the spherical particle volume,
λ
is the wavelength of the absorbing radiation,
ϵ
m
is the dielectric constant of the surrounding medium, and the angular frequency of the exciting radiation,
ϵ
1
and
ϵ
2
are the real and imaginary parts of the material dielectric function, respectively. Herein, we demonstrated a reliable strategy for visual inspection and effective adsorption of heavy metal ions by using the functionalized gold nanoparticles (gold nanoparticles capped by graphite-like nitride doped carbon quantum dots, Au@g-CNQDs) as a sensitive color-tuning indicator to measure the typical weak catalytic heavy metal ions of Cd2+ ions. Due to numerous functional groups like heptazine, carboxyl, and hydroxyl on the surface, Cd2+ ions contact with the surface of Au@g-CNQDs experienced "cooperative effect", realizing the regulation of the nano-interface and achieving the aggregation of Au@g-CNQDs. Therefore, with the existence of Cd2+, the solution color of Au@g-CNQDs exhibits remarkable change, and presents a detection limit of nmol level. This ability to sensitively detection of Cd2+ ions by visual detection provided a robust method for detecting Cd2+ ions in vivo the test at early stage of lung cancer. By simple histochemical staining, we developed a visual sensor of Cd2+ ions method based on the size-dependent optical properties of nanoparticles and investigated the biodistribution and quantitative detection of Cd2+ ions in mice model, which shows tremendous prospects for life-safeguarding (Zhuang et al., 2012). ( Scheme 1)

Urea (CH4N2O, ≥ 99.8%), sodium citrate (C6H5Na3O7, 99%), chloroauric acid hydrated (HAuCl4·4H2O，99.9%), hydrogen nitrate (HNO3, 69%) and hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Various metal nitrate salts including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Cr6+, Hg2+ and Cd2+ were purchased from Aladdin. Nanopure® water (18MΩcm) was made by a Barnstead Nanopure Diamond system. All chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade or above and used as received without further purification.

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HR-TEM) images and the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) patterns were performed using a JEOL 2010F instrument operated at an accelerating voltage 200kV. The morphology and particle height were analyzed by the Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Vecco Nanoscope IIIa, tapping mode). The X-Ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were carried out by using a Rigaku diffractometer D/max-2500. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were recorded on a SKL-12 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was performed on a Nicolet FT-170SX spectrometer. The Atomic Flurescence was recorded by Shimadzu spectrofluorimeter RF-540. The instrument used is an Agilent 7900 ICP-MS instrument (Agilent Technologies, Tokyo, Japan) in the Tianjin University. The ultraviolet and visible (UV–vis) spectra were carried out on the new century spectrophotometer T6. Freezing microtome was performed using in the tissue section for mice (Leica CM1520).

The g-CNQDs were synthesized according to the method reported previously (Zhou et al., 2013). Briefly, 1.01g (16.8mmol) of urea and 0.81g (2.80mmol) of sodium citrate were mixed in an agate mortar (12cm). After the mixture composites grounded 45min, the forming uniform powers were transferred to a poly-(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and then heated to 180°C for 120min in a drying oven. The resultant yellowish product was purified by alternative washing with ethanol and centrifugation at 9000rpm for three times. Then the excess inorganic ions (Na+，C5H5O5COO-) were removed through dialyzing against pure water with a dialysis membrane bag (1000M mass cutoff) for 48h. The obtained precipitate showed excellent water dispersivity.

The Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticle was fabricated by an oxidation-reduction reaction process with N-doped carbon quantum dots (g-CNQDs) as the reducing agent and stabilizer, HAuCl4·4H2O as the gold-source. The details are described as follows.

As shown in scheme S1 in the Supporting information, the Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles were obtained by using the g-CNQDs reduction method. Briefly, 500μL of 30mM HAuCl4 aqueous solution (12.3mg/mL) was quickly added to 30mL of 0.33mg/mLg-CNQDs aqueous solution with intensely stirring. Then, the solution was heated to 120°C for 60min under reflux, and during the heating the corresponded color of the solution changed from pale yellow gradually to pink and then wine-red. After cooling down, the solution was centrifugalized three times at 8500rpm for 20min with deionized water, and then dried by vacuum freeze-drying method. The obtained Au@g-CNQDs was dispersed in water by sonication to forming an aqueous solution of 2.8mg/mL for further research and characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of Cd2+ ions in aqueous solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The naked-eye colorimetric detection of Cd2+ ions with Au@g-CNQDs nanoprobes was performed under ambient temperature. Firstly, aqueous solutions of cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate with different concentrations (ranging from 0.01 to 50μM) were prepared. Secondly, appropriate pH value and amount of NaCl were dropped to the aqueous solution of Au@g-CNQDs (2.8mg/mL), followed by severe shaking to forming the mixture solution. Finally, a given amount of cadmium-ion solution was added into 2mL as-prepared Au@g-CNQDs solution and incubated for 5min. Additionally, if vigorous shaking, the Au@g-CNQDs solution will present remarkable color-changing in less than 30s. The result of colorimetric detection of Cd2+ ions was monitored by the ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrometry where the absorbance ratio of A650nm/A520nm changed dramatically.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on Cd2+ ions biodistribution in mice models
ce:section.ce:para: All animal experiment studies were performed based on the guidelines and approvals of the Chinese Academy of Sciences Institutional Animal Care and use Committee. The Kunming mice (male 22 ± 2g, 7–9 weeks old) were purchased from the Experimental Animal Center of Peking University housed in stainless steel cages under standard environmental conditions with free intake of food and water.

The biodistribution study was performed with 12 mice, which were randomly divided into three groups (n = 4 mice per group). Mice were intravenously administered with 0.01% of Cd2+ ions 3 times (1, 3, and 5 days) to evaluate quantitative tissue distribution and sacrificed after overnight starvation. Fresh samples of liver, kidney, lung, spleen, heart, and brain tissue were collected, fixed and embedded in OCT compound medium (Tissue Tek), then snap-frozen in liquid nitrogen and stored at −80°C for subsequent histological staining of frozen sections (5μm). Frozen tissue sections were incubated with 2.8mg/mL self-synthesized Au@g-CNQDs solution to develop color continued for 30min. After rinsing with double-distilled water, stained tissues were imaged under an Olympus microscopy. The Cd2+ ions extracted from liver, kidney, and lung tissues were made into fluid (2mg/mL), and then quantitative analysis was performed using a real-time UV–vis spectrophotometer.

Considering Cd2+ ions and most of the other tested metal ions are highly toxic and non-degraded, the colorimetric detection of Cd2+ ions should be performed with protective gloves. Besides, to avoid polluting the environment, all the waste solutions were reclaimed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the g-CNQDs, Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots (g-CNQDs) are well monodispersed and have an average diameter of 1.45±0.2nm (Fig. S1 in the Supporting information). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of g-CNQDs exhibit graphitic carbon nitride feature with two characteristic peaks at 13.1° for (100) and 27.4° for (002), corresponding to the interplanar structural packing motif and the long-range interplanar stacking of aromatic systems, respectively (Fig. S2 in the Supporting Information). The corresponding atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Fig. S3 in the Supporting information) image of the g-CNQDs shows the average topographic height is 1.5nm which suggested that the g-CNQDs consist of a single or a few layers of C–N sheets. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image inset in Fig. 1a reveals an average lattice spacing of 0.325nm, consistent with the (002) of graphitic carbon (sp2). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of the g-CNQDs further confirms the composition and chemical structure. The appearance of the N1s peak proves the g-CNQDs were doped with N elements. The N1s region was decomposed into three components centered at 398.8eV, 399.6eV and 400.5eV, assigned to the heptazine heterocyclic ring unit of C˭N-C, C-N-C, and N-(C)3, respectively (Fig. S4b in the Supporting information). For the C1s, the peaks located at 286.1eV, 288.2eV, 530.8eV, and 532.1eV are attributed to C-O, O-C˭O, C˭O, and C-OH/C-O-C, respectively (Fig. S4c in the Supporting information) (Devi et al., 2017; Dong et al., 2013). These existing oxygen-rich carboxyl group and hydroxyl group in the g-CNQDs hence can be used as the reducing agent and surfactant stabilizer.

The crystalline Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles were fabricated by the redox reaction between HAuCl4·4H2O and g-CNQDs (Scheme S1 in the Supporting information). As shown in Fig. 1b, the synthesized Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles were quasi-spherical in shape, discrete, and uniform with an average diameter of 15 ± 1.2nm (the inset of Fig. 1b). The high-magnification TEM image of single Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticle clearly shows that the Au core is coated by an ultrathin "shell" of 1–2nm in thickness. This value is equivalent to the average diameter of g-CNQDs and means the coating composed by a layer of compact g-CNQDs. The corresponding elemental mapping images of single Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticle indicates both Au and N elements are uniformly distributed and covering the whole nanoparticles (Fig. 1d). And the cross-sectional compositional line profiles of individual Au@g-CNQDs further confirms its core–shell structural feature (Fig. S5 in the Supporting information). It can also be confirmed by the XPS spectra of the Au@g-CNQDs. The binding energy of different N1s peaks (Fig. 1e) and C1s (Fig. S4e in the Supporting information) peaks clearly shows the existence of N and C elements at Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles. Compared with g-CNQDs the intensity of the O-C˭O group binding energy collected from the g-CNQDs decreased, which reveals the reduction of O-C˭O peaks during the in situ growth of AuNPs (Fig. S4f in the Supporting information). As such, the Fourier transform-infrared spectrum (FT-IR) characteristic peaks of O-C˭O group at 1592cm−1 becomes distinctly weak after reaction (Zhang and Chen, 2014). The disappearing of the gold nanoparticles peaks are similar to those of g-CNQDs, but the peak at 809cm−1 of heptazine and 1412cm−1 of –C-N stretching vibrations almost disappears. Additionally, both the characteristic peaks of XPS and FT-IR spectra indicate the heptazine, -OH, and -COOH groups abound on the surface of the Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles.

The as-prepared water-soluble Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles has unique optical characteristic. As shown in Fig. 1f, the UV–visible absorption spectrum clearly shows that the g-CNQDs aqueous solution characteristic absorption peak displays at 346nm (
n
→
π
*
) (Gong and Xia, 2016). The Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles have a typical plasmon absorption peak associated with their Au cores at 520nm and forming a wine-red transparent solution. Interestingly, the absorption peak of
π →
π
*
from the heptazine heterocyclic ring unit at 242nm was unchanged indicated that the surface of Au nanoparticles were coated with an ultrathin g-CNQDs layer. On the other hand, the g-CNQDs exhibited strong fluorescence emission centered at 442nm upon excitation at 346nm, the photographs shown in the Fig. 1f inset of the g-CNQDs aqueous solutions are bright violet-blue luminescence under the illumination of a 365nm UV light (Efe et al., 2011). While, for the Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles, it was almost quenched for fluorescence intensity via the photoinduced electron transferred from the g-CNQDs shell to the Au NPs core, indicating that g-CNQDs were steadily capped at Au nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of the visual detection of Cd2+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: With high surface area to volume ratios, the existence of the nanoparticles can be easily regulated by the ions and molecules (Miao et al., 2015; Rachuri et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2015). Herein, by "coordination effect", heavy metal Cd2+ ions can induce the fabricated Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticle aggregation causing the visible color changes obviously. Capped by the g-CNQDs, the fabricated Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles surface contains various types of functional groups such as heptazine, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. These existed hydrophilic groups served as conjugate linkers and effective bound to d10 metal coordination active sites of Cd2+ ions resulting in the Au@g-CNQDs aggregation. Two prominent binding-energy peaks at 412.24eV (assign to Cd 3d3/2) and 405.57eV (assign to Cd 3d5/2) corresponding to Cd 3d10 were observed in Fig. 2a indicating that Au@g-CNQDs nanohybrids contains Cd2+ ions. Additionally, the binding energy of N 1s in Cd/Au@g-CNQDs was shifted from 399.6eV in inorganic-N compounds of Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles) to 400.8eV (Fig. 2b). The generated positive shift of 1.2eV evidenced the interaction between N and Cd2+ in Au@g-CNQDs: electrons were further donated to Cd2+, which makes the N atoms electron-deficient.

According to the Mie theory equation
κ =
18 π N V
ϵ
2
ϵ
m
3 / 2
/
{
λ ×
[
(
ϵ
1
+
2
ϵ
m
)
2
+
ϵ
2
2
]
}
, the color of gold nanoparticles affected by size-dependent optical properties. On account of as-prepared Au@g-CNQDs nanosensors and plasmonic gold nanoparticles aggregation by “coordination effect” in the presence of Cd2+ ions were distinctly changed for size and morphology. As shown in Fig. 2d and e indicated the transmission electron microscopy images on a micron-scale show that the introduction of Cd2+ is more prominent than the absence of Cd2+ for gold nanoparticles aggregation, and the presence of Cd2+ promotes the formation of molecular clusters (Fig. 2c). When nano-particles gather, the surface plasma resonance of single particle is inhibited because of the decrease of the distance between the nanoparticles. At the same time, the gold nanoparticles in the aggregation process are also accompanied by strong plasma resonance absorption band of shift or width change. Based on the size-dependent peak at 520nm gradually decreased, while a new plasma resonance absorption peak appeared at 650nm (Fig. 2f). The red-shift is due to the coupling between the dipole-dipole interaction of electrons and the plasma coupling between adjacent particles in the collective. With the red-shift of the plasma resonant absorption band, the color change of the obvious wine-red to bluish-violet is presented (the inset of Fig. 2d and e).

As shown above, the as-synthesized Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles have distinct size-dependent optical properties and decorated by abundant different functional groups. Notably, with the existence of Cd2+ ions, aqueous solution of the Au@g-CNQDs nanoparticles exhibits significant color changes.

To evaluate the recognition and interference ability of Cd2+ ions, a series of alkali and transition metal cations (10−4 M, 20μL) were added into Au@g-CNQDs solution, under the optimal NaCl concentration of 15mM ( Fig. 3b) and pH value of 6.5 (tris-HCl buffer solution) (Fig. S8 in the Supporting information). The results shown that only Cd2+ ions can induce a distinct color change from wine-red to bluish-violet, corresponding to the reduction of absorbance at 520nm and the emergence of absorbance at 650nm. While for the other metal ions, there was not any obvious change both on the naked-eye colorimetric and UV–vis spectrum, which indicated that the Au@g-CNQDs nanosensors were especially response to Cd2+ ions (Fig. 3a). This Au@g-CNQDs colorimetric nanoprobe also has excellent anti-interference performance for detection of Cd2+ ions. As shown in Fig. 3c, the detectable signal of Cd2+ ions is generally less than 12% variations even under 10-fold of the other metal ions like alkali and transition metal cations (including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Cr6+ and Hg2+ ions) (Guo et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2017b). Therefore, Cd2+ ions gave a remarkably enhanced absorption ratio (A650/A520) at an identical concentration with other metal ions, it can be verified that the Au@g-CNQDs colorimetric nanoprobes toward Cd2+ exhibits unexpected anti-interference ability.

The sensitivity of this operational colorimetric strategy to detection the Cd2+ ions was further evaluated by the real-time UV–vis response. As it shown in Fig. 3d, with increasing the concentration of Cd2+ ions from 0 to 12μM, the intensities of the characteristic peaks at 520nm decreased obviously and simultaneously a new peak at 650nm increased dramatically. Meanwhile, following the increase of the concentration of Cd2+ ions from 0.02 to 2μM, the corresponded visual color gradually changed from wine-red to bluish-violet (Fig. 3e). Additionally, the absorption ratio of A650/A520 to Cd2+ ions concentrations has a good linearity in the range of 0.01μM to 3.0μM (Fig. 3f). Based on the 3σ/slope method, the detection limit was 0.01μM, which was nearly 4×103 times lower than the maximum level 0.5mg/kg (~2.1μM) of Cd2+ ions in drinking water permitted by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) (Gonzalo and Camargo, 2012).

This facile operational colorimetric strategy exhibits great practicality for the detection of Cd2+ ions. Its robustness for the detection of Cd2+ ions both in tap water and lake water was evaluated. Initially, there were no Cd2+ ions detected by both standard inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) method and colorimetric sensor. Then specific concentrations of Cd2+ ions were intentionally spiked in the test samples. As summarized in Table 1, the results acquired by the as-proposed approach were receivable, with the recovery rate of the samples were between 98.0% and 100.4%. Considering the complex water environment in the lake, such results suggested that other coexist species did not disturb the quantification of Cd2+ ions. These results clearly indicate that visual detection method represents an observable potential in real-world detection of Cd2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biological test in mice organ tissues
ce:section.ce:para: Not metabolized or decomposed of toxic heavy metal Cd2+ ions pollution is known as the “invisible killer” in human activities, as it can lead to reduced lung capacity, renal dysfunction, and some cancers (Han et al., 2016). In this paper, by utilizing the fabricated Cd2+ ions sensor, we further assessed the tissue biodistribution of Cd2+ ions in mice. The preliminary experimental results from different organ tissues presented different color-changes distinctly after different times postinjection (1, 3, and 5 times) of Cd2+ ions (Supplementary Fig. S9). After injected continuously for 5 days, dramatic color-changes in liver, kidney, and lung, verified their preferential accumulation of Cd2+ ions (Jiang et al., 2016). The gradual color change of lung, kidney, and liver darken with continuously postinjection of Cd2+ ions, demonstrated that Cd2+ ions could deposited on the tissue quickly (Tjälve et al., 1986). Additionally, for these three organ tissues, the degrees of color change were ordered as liver > kidney > lung, consisting with previously reported liver preferential deposition of Cd2+ ions (Fujita and Kawanishi, 1987). Additionally, this novel sensor could sensing Cd2+ ions from organ tissues ultra-sensitively. As shown in Fig. 4, only tail vein injected once with 60uL (1μM) Cd2+ ions, the stained color of lung tissues changed from wine-red to lilac color. This detectable concentration of Cd2+ ions was about 80nM, which determined by the ICP-MS/UV–vis absorption spectra (Table S1 in the Supporting information) from lung tissue microwave digestion. Thus, with the capability to sensitively visual sensing the biodistribution of Cd2+ ions in vivo environments, this facile strategy provides an important alternative strategy for quickly detection of Cd2+ ions.

In summary, we have developed an innovative strategy to ultra-sensitive and ultra-fast detect weakly colored heavy transition metal Cd2+ ions by using graphite-like nitride doped carbon quantum dots-capped gold nanoparticles. Benefited from the heptazine ring, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups in Au@g-CNQDs surface, through the “cooperative effect”, the extremely trace amount of Cd2+ ions can be absorbed on nanoparticles surface. Combined with the color dependence of nanoparticles aggregation, the Cd2+ ions can be sensitively and quickly captured to detect at nM level nearly at once. Furthermore, the distribution of Cd2+ ions in mice organ tissues was detected precisely and quickly by this facile operational visual sensor, suggesting its observable potential in real applications, i.e., tremendous prospects for clinical detection of Cd2+ ions poisoning.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21575115, 21327005 and 21705117); Program for Chang Jiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team, Ministry of Education, China (Grant No. IRT −16R61).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.12.025.
